coredata.dc:title: Ultraviolet/blue light emitting high-quality graphene quantum dots and their biocompatibility
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Na Eun
Jeong, Jong-Min
Lim, Hyung San
Lee, Sang Yoon
Cho, Sung Oh
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a significant potential in the application to bio-fields. However, most of the GQDs synthesized by existing methods face limitations due to the harmful chemicals involved in their synthesis and the presence of various oxygen functional groups, both of which affect the biocompatibility of the GQDs. Here, we present a simple approach to fabricating fluorescent and biocompatible high-quality GQDs with low oxygen content without the involvement of harmful chemicals. High-quality GQDs are synthesized by hydrogen-assisted pyrolysis of silicon carbide and this dry synthesis process allows the GQDs to possess high crystallinity with no unwanted chemicals on the surface. Subsequently, the high-quality GQDs are slightly oxidized through sonication in ethanol, but the treated GQDs (t-GQDs) still retain their high crystallinity. The t-GQDs exhibit strong ultraviolet/blue light emission with a high yield of 30.9%, a phenomenon that can be attributed to intrinsic emission resulting from the low oxygen content of the t-GQDs. Furthermore, the t-GQDs show low cytotoxicity even at a high concentration of 200 μg/mL due to the absence of harmful chemicals during their synthesis and the low oxygen content of the t-GQDs. These results demonstrate the excellence of the t-GQDs and their applicability in bio-fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
UV/Blue emission
High-quality
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical characterizations of t-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of t-GQDs and HRTEM image of t-GQDs with lattice spacing (inset). (b) Size distribution of t-GQDs. (c) AFM image of t-GQDs with surface topography measurement (length of the yellow line is 1.6 μm).
(a) Raman spectrum and (b) XPS high-resolution C1s spectra of t-GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV absorption spectrum, (b) PL spectrum from 250 nm to 375 nm (inset: t-GQDs prior to and after 365 nm UV light excitation), (c) PL excitation and emission spectrum and (d) PL decay spectrum of t-GQDs dispersed in water.
Schematic illustration of h-GQDs modifying into t-GQDs.

Mouse hepatocytes were treated with t-GQDs at various concentrations (10, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) for 48 h to test for (a) cell viability, levels of extracellular (b) ALT, (c) LDH release from hepatocytes, and (d) measurement of ROS levels after DCF-DA stimulation. (e) Untreated hepatocytes and hepatocytes treated with 200 μg of t-GQDs and CCl4 were observed under the light field microscope. Scale bar is 10 μm long. (f) Fluorescence imaging was done by staining the nucleolus with DAPI (blue) and t-GQDs (green). Scale bar is 10 μm long. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Ultraviolet/blue light emitting high-quality graphene quantum dots and their biocompatibility

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear and Quantum Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 34141, Daejeon, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nuclear and Quantum Engineering
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nuclear and Quantum Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Daejeon, 34141, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anesthesiology, McGovern Medical School, University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston, Houston, 77030, TX, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Anesthesiology, McGovern Medical School, University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston, Houston, TX, 77030, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a significant potential in the application to bio-fields. However, most of the GQDs synthesized by existing methods face limitations due to the harmful chemicals involved in their synthesis and the presence of various oxygen functional groups, both of which affect the biocompatibility of the GQDs. Here, we present a simple approach to fabricating fluorescent and biocompatible high-quality GQDs with low oxygen content without the involvement of harmful chemicals. High-quality GQDs are synthesized by hydrogen-assisted pyrolysis of silicon carbide and this dry synthesis process allows the GQDs to possess high crystallinity with no unwanted chemicals on the surface. Subsequently, the high-quality GQDs are slightly oxidized through sonication in ethanol, but the treated GQDs (t-GQDs) still retain their high crystallinity. The t-GQDs exhibit strong ultraviolet/blue light emission with a high yield of 30.9%, a phenomenon that can be attributed to intrinsic emission resulting from the low oxygen content of the t-GQDs. Furthermore, the t-GQDs show low cytotoxicity even at a high concentration of 200 μg/mL due to the absence of harmful chemicals during their synthesis and the low oxygen content of the t-GQDs. These results demonstrate the excellence of the t-GQDs and their applicability in bio-fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a carbon-based nano-sized material, have attracted notable attention due to their unique properties originating from the quantum confinement effect, such as having a tunable band gap and photoluminescence (PL) [1,2]. Furthermore, the GQDs have lower toxicity and higher water dispersibility than traditional semiconductor quantum dots [3], allowing the former to have potential bio-applications in bio-imaging [4,5], bio-sensing [6,7] and drug delivery [8,9]. Especially, ultraviolet (UV) emission property of the GQDs is important as it expands the applications of GQDs in solar cell fabrication [10,11], energy storage [12] and serves as an important feature for the application in environmental photocatalysis [3,13–15] and organic UV light emitting diodes [16]. However, only a few studies have reported fabricating UV emitting GQDs in the last decade [2,17,18].

Currently, the methods to fabricate GQDs include top-down [7,19–23] and bottom-up approaches [17,24–26]. However, GQDs synthesized by these existing methods have limitations in being used directly in bio-fields due to several issues; the methods involve complicated multistep procedures and harmful chemicals [25] while also causing the fabricated GQDs to have oxygen functional groups. The level of oxygen content in the GQDs is important as it affects the cytotoxicity of the GQDs.

Recently, some reports have suggested that the presence of various oxygen functional groups hinders the biocompatibility of GQDs by inducing oxidative stress [27–29]. GQDs with oxygen functional groups causes formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells [30], which eventually leads to cell deaths [27]. Nonetheless, the presence of oxygen functional groups can also provide advantages by increasing dispersibility in water [31,32], enhancing PL emission [33,34] and accelerating catalytic activity [35]. Thus, it is important to balance the oxygen content of the GQDs to allow adequate dispersibility in water and high PL emission while keeping cytotoxicity in check.

Here, we further expand upon the simple approach to fabricating UV/blue light emitting and biocompatible GQDs with low oxygen content introduced in our earlier research. We have previously synthesized high-quality GQDs (h-GQDs) using hydrogen-assisted pyrolysis of silicon carbide (SiC) without the use of harmful chemicals [36]. The fabricated h-GQDs were free of oxygen and exhibited high crystallinity and purity; however, they were not suitable for use in bio-fields since the h-GQDs were hydrophobic and emitted weak PL signal due to their exceptionally high-quality [37]. In this study, we attached oxygen functional groups to the surfaces of h-GQDs through the simple process of sonication in ethanol to ensure that the GQDs are well dispersed in water [21] and exhibits sufficiently strong PL signal. After the sonication treatment, the oxygen content of the h-GQDs increased slightly to 8% due to the attachment of oxygen functional groups to the surfaces of the particles. The treated GQDs (t-GQDs) exhibit strong UV/blue light emission with a high yield, while maintaining high crystallinity. This notable emission property can be attributed to the intrinsic emission resulting from the ubiquity of isolated sp2 carbon domains generated by the oxygen functional groups [33]. To evaluate the biocompatibility of the t-GQDs for their application in bio-fields, the t-GQDs were analyzed via various in-vitro assays. The results indicated that the t-GQDs possess excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and high stability.

1 cm × 1 cm 4HN–SiC plates with an off-axis angle of 4° from the (0001) basal plane were purchased from TankeBlue Semiconductor Co. (China) and were used to synthesize h-GQDs. Ethanol (≥99.9%) was purchased from Merck chemicals (Germany) and was used to separate GQDs from the SiC plates.

h-GQDs were synthesized via hydrogen assisted pyrolysis of SiC. 4HN–SiC plates were annealed in a vacuum furnace with a mixture gas comprising argon (Ar, 96% v) and Hydrogen (H, 4% v). The internal pressure of the furnace was kept at 80 mTorr by controlling the flow rate of the mixture gas. The temperature inside the furnace was raised to a max temperature of 1500 °C and maintained for 30 min, after which, the furnace was cooled to room temperature. The heating and cooling rates of the furnace were both set to 5 °C per min. The high-quality GQDs formed on the SiC plates were detached from the plates through sonication at 80 kHz in 2 mL of ethanol for 3 min. The separated h-GQDs were centrifuged at 8000 rpm with 10,000 MWCO micro-filters to eliminate large particles. Subsequently, the GQDs dispersed in ethanol were further sonicated for 20 min at 40 °C, after which, the ethanol was vaporized. The dried t-GQDs were washed with deionized water several times to remove any remaining ethanol, then were dispersed in deionized water by sonication for 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterizations of t-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution and lattice spacing of the GQDs were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin, FEI, USA). The thickness of the GQDs were measured with an atomic force microscope (AFM, XE70, Park systems, Republic of Korea). The crystallinity of the GQDs was determined with a micro-Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) equipped with a 514 nm laser source with a spot size of 2 μm.

After sonication treatment in ethanol, the bonding states of dried t-GQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) using Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Instrument, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the dried t-GQDs on a gold substrate were performed using a 3000 W Al Kα as the microfocused monochromatic X-ray source (K-alpha, Thermo VG Scientific, USA). The dried t-GQDs on gold substrate was obtained by placing t-GQDs ethanol dispersion droplets on a gold substrate and vaporizing ethanol in a vacuum chamber. The UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs dispersion were measured with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 1050, PerkinElmer, USA) at room temperature. PL and PL excitation (PLE) spectra were obtained using a fluorimeter (QM-400, HORIBA, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp. PL decay time was determined through time-correlated single photon counting using a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (FL920, Edinburgh Instruments, England). PL quantum yield was determined with an absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer (C11347, Hamamatsu, Japan).

Cytotoxicity of t-GQDs was evaluated by performing various assays on hepatocytes of mice; cell viability assay, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay, and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) assay were performed on hepatocytes treated with t-GQDs. Intracellular ROS generation was measured by determining the intensity of DCF-DA fluorescence after cell stimulation with t-GQDs. Cell imaging of t-GQDs in hepatocytes was carried out with a LEICA DMI 4000B microscope equipped with a DFC450C camera. LEICA CTR 4000 lamp was used as the excitation light source. The details of the cytotoxicity tests are described further in the supplementary data.

h-GQDs were fabricated on the roughened surfaces of SiC plates via hydrogen-assisted pyrolysis of SiC. Since the h-GQDs are hydrophobic due to the hydrogen-terminated edges and aromatic CC bonds, the h-GQDs were completely detached from the plates through sonication in ethanol instead of water [36]. The amount of h-GQDs extracted from a single 1 cm × 1 cm SiC plate was approximately 200 μg. In order to increase the dispersibility of the h-GQDs in water, the h-GQDs were slightly oxidized by further sonication in ethanol for 20 min at 40 °C, after which the ethanol was vaporized. The dried t-GQDs were washed with deionized water several times to remove any remaining ethanol, then were dispersed in deionized water.

The t-GQDs are found to be monodisperse with a size of 2.38 ± 0.32 nm from a TEM image (Fig. 1 a, Fig. 1b). They have a highly-ordered crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (inset of Fig. 1a), a spacing matching that of graphitic plane (020), even after the additional sonication treatment [38,39]. The average thickness of the t-GQDs are ∼1.89 nm (Fig. 1c), measured using an AFM image, indicating that the t-GQDs are composed of a few layers of graphene. The Raman spectra of the t-GQDs (Fig. 2 a) exhibit sharp D (1348 cm−1), G (1582 cm−1) and 2D (2694 cm−1) peaks, indicating that the t-GQDs are composed of a few layers of high crystalline graphene. The D to G peak intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the t-GQDs is 0.93, and the 2D to G peak intensity ratio (I2D/IG) of 0.53 is higher than those achieved in other research [37].

XPS survey spectrum (Supplementary Fig. S1) reveals that the oxygen content of the t-GQDs is 8%, whereas that of the h-GQDs is near zero [36]. High-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of the t-GQDs are deconvoluted into 4 peaks corresponding to the presence of sp2 carbons at 284.4 eV, sp3 carbons at 285.0 eV, C–O groups at 286.2 eV, and CO groups at 287.5 eV (Fig. 2b). The C1 spectrum of h-GQDs shows that the sp2/sp3 carbon ratio is over 9 [36]. After the sonication treatment of the h-GQDs, the sp2 carbons still remain dominant with an integrated peak intensity fraction of 78.44%. However, peaks corresponding to the presence of C–O groups, such as hydroxyl and epoxy groups, and carbonyl (CO) groups appear with integrated peak intensity fractions of 10.19% and 2.54%, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of the t-GQDs supports that epoxy groups (1045, 1260 cm−1) and hydroxyl groups (3315 cm−1) are indeed present in t-GQDs (Supplementary Fig. S2). The peak intensity fraction of sp3 carbons for t-GQDs is 8.84%, and peaks corresponding to C–H bending (1376, 1456 cm−1) and C–H stretching (2854, 2923 cm−1) are all observed in the FT-IR spectrum. The presence of C–H bonds can be attributed to the hydrogen-terminated edges of t-GQDs and the elimination of aromatic CC bonds by sonication [36]. The presence of oxygen functional groups can be attributed to carbon radicals, produced by sp2 carbon bonds at the basal planes of h-GQDs breaking, combining with oxygen. Nevertheless, t-GQDs are still of high crystallinity and high purity, and the defects on t-GQDs are insignificant as shown by Raman and XPS measurements.

The optical properties of t-GQDs were evaluated by UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. UV–vis absorption spectrum of the t-GQDs shows high absorbance of deep UV (<250 nm) with shoulder peaks at 220 and 265 nm (Fig. 3 a), resulting from π-π∗ transition of aromatic C = C bonds [2,17,40,41]. Furthermore, t-GQDs exhibit an absorption band at 325 nm, which can be attributed to n-π∗ transition, indicating that oxygen functional groups are passivated on the surfaces of t-GQDs [33,41,42].

PL spectra of t-GQDs in deionized water excited at various wavelengths is shown in Fig. 3b. As opposed to h-GQDs, t-GQDs show significant PL emission in UV/blue region. h-GQDs exhibit immeasurably low PL signal due to poor dispersibility in water and their high-quality and high purity making quantum confinement effect the sole way to produce PL [37].

The inset of Fig. 3b shows bright blue light being emitted from t-GQDs dispersed in water excited with 365 nm UV light in a darkroom. The t-GQDs show the most intense UV/blue light emission with a quantum yield of 30.9% when they are excited by 300 nm light. The shape of the PL spectrum of the t-GQDs is independent of the excitation wavelength; the positions of the PL peaks remain constant even when the excitation wavelength is changed. t-GQDs exhibit 5 emission peaks at 333, 348, 358, 382 and 402 nm. The strong UV emission property of the t-GQDs is very noteworthy, as only a few studies have reported fabricating UV emitting GQDs in the last decade [2,17,18].

The PL intensities of t-GQDs are dependent on the sonication time (Supplementary Fig. S3). The PL intensities increase with increasing sonication time until 20 min of sonication is reached; past 20 min, the PL intensities remain relatively constant. Thus, the optimal sonication time was determined to be 20 min as further sonication will cause needless oxidization.

Intense UV/blue light emission from t-GQDs was also observed with t-GQDs ethanol dispersion (Supplementary Fig. S4). The PL emission peaks of t-GQDs in ethanol are located at similar positions to those of t-GQDs in water. However, unlike the emission curve of t-GQDs in water, that of t-GQDs in ethanol exhibit excitation wavelength dependent behavior.

The PLE spectrum of t-GQDs shows two broad peaks centered at 290 and 343 nm (Fig. 3c). The energy difference between these two PLE peaks is comparable to that between the two UV absorption peaks centered at 265 and 325 nm. The intensity of the PLE peak signifying n-π∗ transition at 343 nm being lower than that signifying π -π∗ transition at 290 nm indicates that the defect density is not particularly high. The time-resolved PL decay spectrum was collected using the time-correlated single photon counting technique, and is shown in Fig. 3d. The fast decay time (τ1) and the slow decay time (τ2) were found to be 3.78 ns are 10.52 ns, respectively, supporting that t-GQDs do exhibit high fluorescent quantum yield.

Fig. 4 is a schematic illustration of h-GQDs modifying into t-GQDs. h-GQDs are composed of aromatic CC with hydrogen-terminated edges and no oxygen content as they were formed from pyrolysis of SiC at high temperature [36]. Defect sites are created throughout the surfaces of h-GQDs during sonication in ethanol at 40 °C [43]. At this time, carbon radicals at the defect sites react with ethanol to form hydroxyl or epoxy bonds, resulting in numerous isolated sp2 carbon domains existing within the t-GQDs. The oxygen functional groups generated on the surfaces of the GQDs as shown in Fig. 2b enhance dispersibility of t-GQDs in water. Additionally, some of the aromatic sp2 bonds on the surfaces of the h-GQDs are destroyed by the sonication process, increasing the proportion of sp3 bonds present as shown in FT-IR spectrum.

The intense UV/blue light emission property of t-GQDs can be explained by the intrinsic emission resulting from isolated sp2 carbon domains in the t-GQDs [33,44] as shown in Fig. 4. For slightly-oxidized GQDs, intrinsic emission is the dominant mechanism for PL, resulting in an intense light emission at wavelengths near 400 nm [33]. Numerous isolated sp2 carbon domains produced by oxygen functional groups create various discrete sp2 energy levels between π and π∗ states [33,45–47] (Supplementary Fig. S5). All of these phenomena explain the strong UV/blue light emission property of the t-GQDs.

The long-term stability and photostability of the t-GQDs were evaluated (Supplementary Fig. S6) through PL intensity observation. For long-term stability evaluation of the t-GQDs, the PL intensity of the t-GQDs dispersed in water was measured at various times over a period of 60 days (Supplementary Fig. S6a). Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) between aged t-GQDs (F) and fresh t-GQDs (F0) remains relatively constant up to 5 days, but the ratio tends to decrease after 5 days.

The PL intensity of the t-GQDs, under continuous UV irradiation (15W, 365 nm wavelength), decreased linearly with time. F/F0 of t-GQDs irradiated for 1 and 2 h were 0.77 and 0.57, respectively. The result indicates that the t-GQDs dispersed in water are oxidized and decomposed when exposed to UV light, reducing the PL intensity [33]. Nonetheless, as shown in the inset of Supplementary Fig. S6b, the t-GQDs still exhibit intense PL emission even after 2 h of UV exposure, indicating that the photostability of the t-GQDs will not be a significant issue for bio-imaging applications.

Cytotoxicity and stability of the fabricated t-GQDs in living cells were investigated using various in-vitro assays. It has been previously reported that the GQDs accumulate in the livers of mice via the vascular circulation systems [48], therefore, primary hepatocytes of mice are suitable for use in analyzing the cytotoxicity of GQDs. Freshly isolated hepatocytes of mice were directly treated with varying concentrations (10, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) of t-GQDs for 48 h. As shown in Fig. 5 a, treatments with t-GQDs using concentrations of up to 200 μg/mL did not significantly affect cell viability in comparison to the control (0 μg/mL) treatment. The measurements of ALT and LDH levels of t-GQDs (Fig. 5b and c) further supported the findings obtained from the cell viability tests; the increase in levels of ALT and LDH resulting from increase in concentration of t-GQDs were negligible.

The rate of ROS production in cells when treated with t-GQDs was investigated to evaluate the effect of t-GQDs on cells. ROS is considered to play a role in causing cellular toxicity by inducing inflammatory responses and negatively impacting other important biological processes [27]. When the ROS level rises inside a cell, the cell incurs oxidative stress, which can ultimately lead to cellular death [49]. To confirm the cytotoxic effect of t-GQDs, the level of intracellular ROS present in hepatocytes treated with t-GQDs after DCF-DA stimulation was measured. Fig. 5d shows that increase in ROS level with increasing concentration of t-GQDs is negligible, indicating that the exposure to t-GQDs does not affect the immune system of hepatocytes. Furthermore, microscopic observations revealed that hepatocytes treated with t-GQDs (200 μg/mL) retained normal cellular morphology, whereas those treated with carbon tetrachloride (CCl4, 20 mM), a known toxic chemicals to human, became severely malformed and appeared to be in the process of cell termination (Fig. 5e). The low cytotoxicity of the t-GQDs can be attributed to the fact that no harmful chemicals were involved in the synthesis process and the oxygen content of the t-GQDs was very low [48,50]. Collectively, these results strongly suggest that t-GQDs are non-toxic, highly biocompatible, and applicable to living cells.

To show that the use of t-GQDs is appropriate for cellular imaging, we acquired in-vitro cellular images of primary hepatocytes treated with t-GQDs. The images, seen in Fig. 5f, show the nuclei stained in blue with DAPI, and areas containing t-GQDs, surrounding the nuclei, illuminated in green. Since the fluorescence of DAPI is generally blue in color, we focused on the green light emission from t-GQDs, instead of the blue light emission, to observe the distribution of t-GQDs and to distinguish the areas of the distribution from the nuclei. Additionally, from the images, it can be observed that the uptake of t-GQDs primarily takes place in the cytoplasm rather than in the nuclei of the hepatocytes. These results support the use of t-GQDs in high contrast bio-imaging and in other biomedical fields.

A summary of the reported properties of GQDs prepared by top-down and bottom-up approaches is listed in Table S1. The GQDs in the list exhibit blue to red PL emission and those with a high quantum yield possess a high O/C ratio. The GQDs fabricated in this study show a relatively high quantum yield while possessing the lowest O/C ratio and highest cell viability among the listed GQDs; the high cell viability can be attributed to the low oxygen content. The excellence in performance and low oxygen content of the GQDs fabricated in this study make them highly suitable for applications in biofields.

In summary, we have presented a simple approach to fabricating fluorescent and biocompatible high-quality GQDs with low oxygen content by employing hydrogen-assisted pyrolysis of SiC followed by sonication in ethanol. The t-GQDs possess high crystallinity and a low oxygen content of 8%, allowing for a strong UV/blue light emission with a high yield of 30.9%. Furthermore, the t-GQDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility due to the absence of harmful chemicals and the low oxygen content of the t-GQDs. From these findings, we believe that our proposed approach will be highly applicable in biomedical fields involving bio-imaging and drug delivery.

S.O.C. designed and supervised the research. N.E.L., H.Y.L. and S.Y.L. prepared the sample and did AFM, XPS and Raman measurement. N.E.L. did SEM, TEM and HRTEM. N.E.L and H.S.L did FT-IR. J.M.J. did cytotoxicity test and measure ROS generation. S.O.C, N.E.L and J.M.J prepared the manuscript. All authors commented on the manuscript. N.E.L. and J.M.J. contributed equally to this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (NRF- 2020M2D8A2069727).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Experimental details of cytotoxicity test of the t-GQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey spectrum of t-GQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of t-GQDs, PL spectra of t-GQDs according to sonication time in ethanol, PL spectra of t-GQDs in ethanol excited at various wavelengths from 250 nm to 375 nm, energy band diagram of t-GQDs, time evolution of PL intensity of a GQDs aqueous dispersion, time evolution of PL intensity of GQDs aqueous dispersion under irradiation with a UV lamp (15W) at 365 nm, and summary of reported properties of GQDs prepared by top-down and bottom-up methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.08.015.
